topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting irritation. I stared at the screen, the numbers stark and unforgiving. Sarah, the new hire, had just received her quarterly bonus notification, and the figure dwarfed mine by a ridiculous margin. A tight knot tightened in my chest, a hot, prickly sensation that made my jaw clench. I watched her smugly tap the keyboard, a picture of undeserved success. Every shared glance between her and the senior management felt like a deliberate slight, a confirmation of everything I silently resented about her presence. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my suddenly clammy palms, and took a scalding sip, the bitterness mirroring the taste of my growing resentment.

[story 2]

My stomach churned, a slow, nauseating twist that made it hard to focus on the spreadsheets in front of me. When I finally saw the pay stub comparison, the air seemed to thin out. It wasn't just the difference in salary; it was the implication—that my years of dedication, the late nights I’d sacrificed, meant less than some fresh-faced newcomer. I slammed the file shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet cubicle. My hands shook slightly as I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the wheels on the linoleum sounding like an accusation. I needed to regain control, to shove this unwelcome realization down into the darkest corner of my mind before it consumed everything.

[story 3]

I leaned back in my ergonomic chair, the leather groaning under my weight. My breath came in short, shallow bursts. Seeing the figures laid out across the monitor felt like a physical assault. A deep, cold anger began to pool in my core, settling low and heavy. I forced myself to breathe, trying to keep my expression neutral, but my knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush that had nothing to do with the temperature of the room. I just wanted to erase the sight, to somehow make the disparity disappear, even if only in my own perception.
